content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 15, 2006


Debbi Cotton
Ocean View School District Board Member
17200 Pinehurst Lane
Huntington Beach, CA 92647

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-06-019

Dear Ms. Cotton:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding your duties as a
Ocean View School District Board Trustee under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

As a member of the Ocean View School District Board of Trustees, do you have a
conflict of interest in a decision on the teachers' union contract and teacher salaries where
you have received at least $500 in income from a teacher in the district?

CONCLUSION

You will have a conflict of interest in the contract and salary decisions if the
decision will affect your source of income by $1,000 or more.

FACTS

You are a Trustee on the Ocean View School District Board of Trustees. Within
the last twelve months you have received a payment in the amount of $800.00 for legal
services rendered prior to your election in November 2004. The payment was from a
teacher in your school district and therefore relevant for conflict purposes. You
anticipate the vote on the teachers' union contract will come before the Board of Trustees
during the twelve month window. This contract will involve salary as an issue. As such,

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-
18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-06-019
Page No. 2

you are concerned that your voting on the teacher's contract will have a financial impact
on the teacher.

ANALYSIS

"The primary purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them." (§ 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal,
section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. Determining whether a conflict of interest
exists under section 87100 requires an analysis under the steps outlined below.

Steps One and Two: Are you considered a "public official" making, participating in
making, or influencing a governmental decision?

A "public official" is broadly defined as any "member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." As a boardmember of the Ocean View
School District Board of Trustees, you are a public official subject to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act. (§ 82048; reg. 18701(a).)

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the scope of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See reg. 18702.1.) You anticipate you will
"make a governmental decision" regarding the teachers' union contract.

Step Three: What are your economic interests – the possible sources of a conflict of
interest?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (§ 87103(a); reg.
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management (§ 87103(d); reg. 18703.1(b));

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (§ 87103(b); reg. 18703.2);

File No. A-06-019
Page No. 3

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (§ 87103(c); reg. 18703.3);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(§ 87103(e); reg. 18703.4);

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (§ 87103; reg. 18703.5).

Your facts identify two economic interests. First, you presumably have an
investment of $2,000 or more in your law practice. (§ 87103(a) & (c).) In addition, you
have an economic interest in each of your customers from whom you have received
income aggregating to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the time the decision will
be made.² (§ 87103(c).)

You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests,
therefore, for purposes of this letter, we assume that you have no other economic interests
relevant to the decision you have identified.

Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?

Law Practice/Clients

A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A

² Income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in
which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially a 10 percent interest or greater.
Income received twelve months prior to a decision may trigger disqualification, even if it is not reportable.
This letter does not address whether or not the payment is reportable on a statement of economic interests.
If you wish to assert the attorney/client privilege in connection with reporting the name of the source of
income on your statement of economic interest (Form 700), you should follow the procedure set forth in
regulation 18740.

File No. A-06-019
Page No. 4

person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person." (Reg. 18704.1(a).)

Neither your business nor your clients initiated the decision in question.
Consequently, your law practice and customers would be indirectly involved in the
decision in question.

Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?

Law Practice

If a business entity is not directly involved in a governmental decision, the
materiality standard of regulation 18705.1(c), applies. (Reg. 18704.1(b).) Subdivision
(c)(4) applies to small business entities that are not publically traded. Pursuant to this
materiality standard, the financial effect of a government decision on a small business
(such as the law practice of a sole practitioner) is material if it is reasonably foreseeable
that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in the increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more." (Reg. 18705.1(c)(4).)

Clients

The effect of a decision is material as to an individual who is a source of income
to an official and indirectly involved in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that any
of the following will occur:

"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income,
investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than
real property) by $1,000 or more; or

"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest
in a manner that is considered material under Title 2, California Code of
Regulations, section 18705.2(b)." (Reg. 18705.3(b)(3).)

File No. A-06-019
Page No. 5

You have not provided specific facts concerning the terms of a proposed teachers'
contract. However, if the salary and benefits of your source of income will be affected by
$1,000 or more by the decision, the effect will be considered material.

Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon your economic interests will meet the applicable materiality
standard?

After identifying the applicable materiality standards, you must assess whether it
is reasonably foreseeable that one or more of the materiality standards will be met. If so,
then you will have a conflict of interest and will be prohibited from participating in the
decision. An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Reg. 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Law Practice

It does not appear that your law practice will be foreseeably affected by the
decision on the teachers' contract. However, because the Commission does not act as a
finder of fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), the
determination of whether it is or is not reasonably foreseeable that this decision will
materially affect your business is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately for you
to decide.

Clients

You note that one of your clients (a source of income of at least $500) is a teacher
subject to the new contract. Consequently, it is foreseeable that the contract decision will
affect this source of income.

Steps Seven and Eight: The "Public Generally" and "Legally Required
Participation" Exceptions

Even if a public official determines that his or her economic interest will
experience a material financial effect as a result of a decision before the official, he or she
may still participate if the decision affects a significant segment of the public generally
and the governmental decision will financially affect the public official's interest in
substantially the same manner as it will affect the significant segment identified. The
"legally required participation" rule applies when the official's participation in a
governmental decision is legally required. (Section 87101; reg. 18708, copy enclosed.)

File No. A-06-019
Page No. 6

You have not provided any facts indicating that either exception applies to the
decision in question.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

QUESTION: As a member of the Ocean View School District Board of Trustees, do you have a
conflict of interest in a decision on the teachers' union contract and teacher salaries where
you have received at least $500 in income from a teacher in the district?

CONCLUSION: You will have a conflict of interest in the contract and salary decisions if the
decision will affect your source of income by $1,000 or more.
